data.title: Spartan Delta: Putting The Success Odds In Your Favor
data.description:
Many years ago, when I first began writing articles for the website, there was a huge interest in having me write about energy stocks that did not separate investors from their money, as is extremely common in this industry. The very extreme case was a stock I wrote about years ago that quadrupled in price one week before it filed for bankruptcy (roughly). The other thing I wrote about was "The Big Discovery Trap" which very often happens with very small companies. Spartan Delta (DALXF) has long been a way to avoid a lot of the "traps" in this industry, as the last article noted.

The Record

This management has long been known for building and then selling substantial parts of the company to essentially begin over again.

A company like this one is suitable for new investors in the energy industry because this management has a fairly long history of success, as shown above.

Probably the most interesting takeaway from managements like this one is that many investors believe that a lot of debt is necessary for a "homerun" in this part of the stock market. But most of the managements like this one that are very successful generally keep the debt levels to a very conservative level. Unlike a very common investor attitude, most managements consider debt a very seldom used tool.

But the major idea is that successful (or "great") investors often center upon repeatable results (and consistency). Whereas much of the time I run into the attitude of "How do I get every last penny?"

Rather than get every last penny from the current idea, I tend to look for results, as shown above, that are far more likely to avoid big losses.

Logan Energy

As is often the case with professionals that build and sell (either companies or parts of companies), this management decided that a certain proposal was different enough that a separate company, Logan Energy (LOECF) was formed and spun off. Both companies are Canadian operators that report using Canadian dollars. The only difference is the location chosen for operations.

Evidently, management considered the location considerations different enough to justify a separate company. But again, the same management shown above that made all those returns to shareholders also runs this company.

That puts the odds in favor of the investor when many similar-sized companies will not be around a few years later.

The Business

As was noted in the annual earnings press release in February, the remaining operations had the main goal of producing from an interval that produced rich gas. These leases had long produced dry gas. But newer production techniques due to advancing technology had now made a different interval competitive.

As a result of this shift in emphasis, oil production grew tremendously, as shown above. That is very likely to lower the corporate breakeven point in the long run as well as increase profitability.

Map Of Operations

This company is at the rapidly growing stage. Generally, whenever a decision is made to sell some operations or spin off some part of the company into a new company, there is a beginning budget made.

As of right now, there would appear to be a budget that will keep production level with the past fiscal year. But there is also a goal to increase the more valuable liquids production as the natural gas production decreases.

That means that even though production per share may not increase, profitability is very likely to increase.

It can be a bit scary to watch experienced management "slap a company together" rapidly because oftentimes, in the beginning, cash flow is very negative. But a management as successful as this one knows what it is doing and has a final goal in mind.

Most of these managements will switch to a "live within means" model that may or may not include a dividend in the future. Therefore, out-of-control cash spending is generally not the issue here as it is elsewhere.

Nonetheless, it does not hurt to watch the debt levels "just in case".

Summary

A company like Spartan Delta is one that is suitable for a wide variety of investors. However, new investors may want to focus on a company like this one to learn about the industry in a relatively safe way. This management is going to do a lot of things right that can then be used to compare against other industry ideas.

What further simplifies things is that generally a stock like this can be held until management sells the company. This helps beginning investors to determine when the time comes to buy the industry and then when to sell the industry.

When a management like this one jumps back into the industry (as several have in other articles I wrote), then obviously it is time to consider purchasing shares of companies. Similarly, when a management sells part or all of the company, as was done a couple of years back, then it is time to see if that was a sell signal by people that really know the industry.

In the rare event that the stock price clearly gets ahead of itself, it can be sold and then repurchased on the inevitable pullback. Right now, we seem to be far away from an overpriced stock, though.

The finances here are rock solid with a very conservative debt level. That is typical for a management with this level of experience.

Risks

A severe and sustained commodity price downturn might well cause me to re-evaluate the prospects of the company. But it should also be considered that the conservative debt level combined with management experience would likely cause a lot less suffering here than is the case elsewhere in the industry.

One of the reasons for the low debt levels is the very volatile stock prices. Future stock prices are also considered to have very low visibility. That makes financial leverage extra dangerous. Instead, a management like this is likely to go for an operational advantage by finding above-average geology to work with. Then the company will be far better suited to withstanding an extremely nasty cyclical downturn.

Similarly, there are many companies that announce a "big discovery". But North America is one of the most explored continents in the world. Therefore, the location of the oil is often known by the industry well ahead of any "discovery" announcement. Rather than relying upon reserves, investors need to concentrate on production costs, cash flow, and amounts recoverable. Often times, smaller companies' "big discovery announcements" are followed up with disappointing production results (and high costs).

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Iran: Not Close To Over Yet
data.description:
Thanks, mostly to Wednesday's big rally, the major indices had their best week since November. The S&P 500 added 3.6% this week, the Nasdaq climbed 4.7%, and the Dow, advanced three percent. With last week's rise, all the major indexes are now close to where they started 2026, despite the huge uptick in volatility.

Last week's trading action was a great example of how complacent investors have become despite growing threats to the U.S. economy and equity markets. The big rise in stocks last week was triggered by a two-week ceasefire announced at the 11th hour Tuesday between the U.S. and Iran. To call this ceasefire 'fragile' is overly generous. The agreement supposedly consisted of a cessation of hostilities combined with the opening of the Strait of Hormuz.

There is already a divergence of opinion between the U.S. and Iran on whether this ceasefire included Lebanon, where Israel has launched their biggest bombing missions in decades in order to root out Iran's proxy Hezbollah. And very few ships have gone through the Strait of Hormuz, with only a handful of Iran‑linked and/or Chinese vessels transiting this chokepoint. An insignificant number, given over 800-900 vessels are backed up waiting to go through this transit point. Roughly half of which are oil tankers.

And while talks in Pakistan will be the highest-level face-to-face talks between Tehran and Washington in years, given the apparent gulf between the two sides, it is hard to be overly optimistic of a permanent resolution being reached. My own view is that it is basically a coin flip which way these discussions play out.

I could easily see these discussions leading to nowhere. Both sides will use the two-week ceasefire to rearm, and hostilities will break out again as soon as the ceasefire period ends, if not before. And that scenario is not priced into the markets. Interestingly, the oil markets, after posting their biggest daily decline in six years on Wednesday, did little the rest of the week. One would expect oil would have plunged further if investors believed a long-term solution was in the offing. 10-year Treasury yields also should have dropped if there was optimism around peace talks, but that simply hasn't happened. Therefore, my view is markets believe this conflict isn't nearly over yet.

And even if peace broke out next week, a lot of damage has already been done to both the U.S. and global economy. It may be already too late to avoid a recession or at least a bout of stagflation. And these signs are increasingly present. March consumer sentiment plunged last week to historical lows, with one-year inflation expectations rising a full 100 bps from the previous month to 4.8%.

And speaking of fragile, there was a cacophony of voices noting the increasing probabilities of recession and/or stagflation for the U.S. economy last week. Mark Zandi, the chief economist at Moody’s Analytics, stated that recession risks have become “uncomfortably high” as the U.S. economy is now “very fragile” and teetering on the edge of a downturn. Mr. Zandi also unveiled a new recession indicator he dubbed the “Vicious Cycle Index,” which is currently already pointing to a recession. This indicator is basically the classic Sahm Rule modified to account for labor participation rates. Not as catchy as the 'Misery Index' developed during the last big bout of stagflation in the U.S. in the late 1970s, but a metric to keep an eye on.

Mr. Zandi was hardly the only pundit in the Stagflation corner last week. The IMF warned last week that the ongoing conflict in Iran will 'slow global growth, raise inflation, and worsen food insecurity globally.' Not to be outdone, the head of U.S. equity and quantitative strategy at BofA Securities noted that current market indicators pointed to a 'classic stagflationary market environment.'

Investors are already seeing the early signs of an inflationary surge. Friday's CPI reading rose 0.9% on a month-over-month basis in March. This was up from 0.3% in February. March's CPI level was up 3.3% over the prior 12 months. This was up significantly from 2.4% in February. Gasoline prices surged just over 21% during the month, which accounted for nearly three-quarters of the CPI increase from February.

Diesel prices have risen more than 50% so far in 2026. That increases logistical costs across the entire economy, as trucks and trains use diesel to move goods to market. The price of jet fuel in the U.S. has also roughly doubled. It has moved from $2.50 a gallon on Feb. 27 to $4.88 a gallon on April 2. It has fallen back a bit since then. United Airlines (UAL) and other airlines have raised fees to help offset costs and have started to raise fares. A jet fuel shortage could trigger mass cancellations in Europe if not relieved over the next three weeks.

In summary, I view last week's rise as little more than a short-lived relief rally. The probabilities of new hostilities breaking out in the Middle East by the end of April appear to be at least 50/50. The chances of the Strait of Hormuz being fully reopened are much, much lower than that. Simply put, this isn't over yet. Nowhere close, in fact. And the longer this global chokepoint remains effectively closed, the greater the likelihood of recession or stagflation.

Neither scenario is properly priced into the markets. Like most investors, I was happy to see green across most of my portfolio last week after some rough going for equities in March. That said, I did little trading within my portfolio. I rolled some of my covered call holdings forward and added a bit to my gold mining positions. At the end of the week, my portfolio was roughly 30% in short-term treasuries and cash and 70% in covered call holdings around the few stocks in the market I see as attractively or reasonably priced. If hostilities renew in the weeks ahead, there will be ample opportunities to use some of that 'dry powder' to act upon lower entry points in the market.

data.title: VICI Properties: A Winning High-Yield Bet To Buy Now
data.description:
In an environment of market volatility, many may feel forced to choose between the safety of high-yield income and the excitement of capital appreciation. In my view, the most resilient portfolios bridge this gap by selecting compounders. Businesses with a stable floor of cash flow and the infrastructure for future growth.

One opportunity sits at the intersection of trophy real estate and disciplined financial engineering. Sporting a perfect rent collection record and a multi-year path for growth, this investment-grade REIT is trading at a valuation that implies the market is overlooking its potential.

Of course, I’m talking about VICI Properties (VICI). When I last covered VICI with a Buy rating in December, I thought that the net lease REIT’s growth prospects were intact. VICI’s balance sheet was investment-grade. Shares were also a compelling value.

Four months later, I’m reiterating my Buy rating. Alongside its annual lease escalators, VICI’s expanded relationship with Cain and Eldridge Industries, as well as the acquisition of Pure Casino Entertainment’s assets, are tangible growth catalysts. VICI’s net leverage ratio was at the low end of its targeted leverage ratio. Finally, shares remain attractively valued.

More AFFO Growth to Come

On February 25th, VICI shared its earnings report for the fourth quarter ended December 31st, 2025. The company’s total revenue increased by 3.8% over the year-ago period to $1.01 billion in the quarter. Adjusting for a 1.2% increase in the share count, VICI’s revenue grew solidly at a low single-digit clip on a per-share basis during the quarter.

What contributed to the net lease REIT’s revenue growth for the fourth quarter? Most of VICI’s total revenue growth was made possible by a 6.7% uptick in income from lease financing receivables to $448.77 million in the quarter. Contributing factors behind this higher revenue included the initial $450 million mezzanine loan investments tied to the development of One Beverly Hills, a landmark 17.5-acre luxury mixed-use development in Beverly Hills, California, and the up to $510 million in delayed draw term loans for the development of the North Fork Mono Casino & Resort.

VICI’s income from sales-type leases edged 1.9% higher over the year-ago period to $534.65 million during the fourth quarter. That was due to contractual escalators embedded in VICI’s existing leases and reinvestment-driven rent growth at the Venetian Resort Las Vegas.

These more than offset slight declines in other income and golf revenue for VICI for the fourth quarter (down 3% to $18.88 million and 3.2% to $10.79 million, respectively).

The net lease REIT’s AFFO per share rose by 5.3% year-over-year to $0.60 in the fourth quarter. The combination of disciplined management of costs and $2.1 billion of capital commitments in 2025 helped VICI’s AFFO margin expand by 180 basis points to 63.4% during the quarter. This is how its AFFO per share growth outpaced revenue growth for the quarter.

Looking ahead, there are plenty of reasons to like VICI.

For one, 46% of the rent roll is subject to CPI-linked escalation in 2026. By 2035, this will rise to 90% of the rent roll. That provides a built-in floor for AFFO per share growth.

Then, there are the deals that VICI has been executing lately to drive further growth. Late last month, the company increased its One Beverly Hills Mezzanine loan to $1.5 billion alongside JPMorgan’s (JPM) $2.8 billion senior loan commitment. This is an additional $1.05 billion incremental commitment beyond VICI’s $450 million investment in the project. While not explicitly mentioned, the interest rate is likely in the range of 8% to 9%.

More importantly, this isn’t just a passive loan. The mezzanine loan gives VICI a front-row seat to eventually acquire the real estate of the Aman Beverly Hills and associated luxury retail/residential components if Cain and Eldridge ever seek to monetize the assets.

Another deal that VICI recently announced was a 200.6 million CAD ($144.4 million) purchase of the real estate assets of Deerfoot Inn & Casino, Great Northern Casino, and two limited-service hotels adjacent to the Great Northern Casino located in Alberta, Canada. This deal with Pure Casino Entertainment represents an 8% cap rate (with a 25-year initial base lease term and four five-year tenant renewal options). The portfolio rent will escalate at 1% on the first February 1st following a full 12 months post-closing, and the escalation thereafter will be the greater of 1.5% or the change in Canadian CPI (capped at 2.5%). Like the Golden Entertainment acquisition that I outlined in my previous article, this acquisition is also expected to close in mid-2026.

For these reasons, the FAST Graphs analyst consensus is for AFFO per share to grow by 2.9% in 2026 to $2.45. In 2027, an additional 3.3% jump in AFFO per share to $2.53 is being projected. For 2028, another 3.2% increase in AFFO per share to $2.61 is the current consensus.

VICI is financially stable, too. As of December 31st, 2025, the net lease REIT’s LQA net leverage ratio was 5.0x. This is at the low end of its targeted net leverage ratio of between 5x and 5.5x. The debt maturity schedule is also well-laddered, with no more than $2 billion in unsecured debt coming due in any given year over the next decade. That’s why VICI enjoys BBB- credit ratings from S&P and Fitch with stable outlooks, as well as a Baa3 (BBB- equivalent) credit rating from Moody’s with a stable outlook (unless otherwise sourced or hyperlinked, all info in this subhead was according to VICI’s Q4 2025 Earnings Press Release and VICI’s March 2026 Investor Presentation).

Attractive Upside to Fair Value

Since December, VICI’s shares have returned 4%. Over that same time, the S&P 500 index (SP500) was flat. Even after this modest rally, the net lease REIT looks to be as compelling a value now as it was four months ago.

Shares are trading at a forward 12-month P/AFFO ratio of 11.4, which is in line with where the valuation was in December. For more perspective, this is much less than the eight-year average P/AFFO ratio of 15.3.

Moving forward, I believe that a reasonable fair value multiple for VICI is around 14. That would be nearly one standard deviation below the average P/AFFO ratio.

This accounts for the fact that VICI’s growth profile looks to be intact. That’s evidenced by the net lease REIT’s 3% to 4% annual AFFO per share growth consensus.

The only factor preventing VICI from fully returning to its eight-year average P/AFFO ratio is the interest rate environment. The 4.3% 10-year U.S. Treasury yield (US10Y) is much higher than the 2.9% average over the past eight years, per data from Macrotrends. This is how I arrive at my fair value P/AFFO ratio of 14.

Currently, the calendar year 2026 is 27% in the books. That means another 73% of 2026 and 27% of 2027 are still ahead within the coming 12 months. This produces a forward 12-month AFFO per share input of $2.47.

Plugging my fair value P/AFFO multiple of 14 into this input, I compute a fair value of $35 a share. Against the current $28 share price (as of April 10th, 2026), this is equivalent to a 19% discount to fair value.

If VICI matches growth forecasts and reverts to fair value, it could post a 31% total return (32% annualized) by the end of March 2027. Through 2031, the net lease REIT could deliver 11% annual total returns.

The Payout Can Keep Rising

VICI’s 6.4% forward dividend yield is approximately 150 basis points higher than the 4.9% median forward yield of the real estate sector. That represented a 10 basis point narrowing of the spread since December. This is how VICI earns respective B+ and A- grades from Seeking Alpha’s Quant System for forward dividend yield and overall dividend yield (both of which were unchanged from December).

Since its IPO, VICI has raised its payout each year. That gives it a 7-year dividend growth streak, which is well above the sector median of 1.8 years. This is why the Quant System issues a B+ grade for overall dividend consistency (up from a B grade in December).

The net lease REIT’s payout is also reasonably safe. That’s supported by an AFFO payout ratio poised to be in the mid-70% range for 2026. Per the Zen Research Terminal, this would be better than the 90% payout ratio set forth by rating agencies for the industry.

As a result, the Zen Research Terminal pegs the probability of a payout cut at 1.3% within the next 12 months. In the next average recession, this rises to 4.5%. That’s why I believe the A- grade from the Quant System for overall dividend safety is just about on the money (up from a B+ grade in December).

The Quant System anticipates 3.8% forward dividend per share growth from VICI. For more context, this is moderately above the sector median of 2.7%. This accounts for the Quant System’s B- grade for overall dividend growth (down from a B grade in December).

Risks to Consider

While VICI is set up for continued AFFO per share growth, it does face risks that could alter the investment thesis.

The key risk to the net lease REIT is its tenant concentration. VICI derived almost three-quarters of its cash rent (73%) from Caesars Entertainment (CZR) (39%) and MGM Resorts International (MGM) (34%). If either tenant faces a credit downgrade, VICI’s stock would be collateral damage in such a scenario.

Fortunately, VICI’s recent series of deals shows that it is laser-focused on broadening and further diversifying its tenant base. In the meantime, the most pressing risk to the net lease REIT is the ongoing lease negotiations with Caesars for its Regional Master Lease, which makes up around a quarter of VICI’s net operating income. Any rent reductions or less favorable escalation terms could set a precedent that may spook investors across the entire portfolio.

Another concentration risk to VICI is its dependence on Las Vegas. As of December 31st, 2025, 48% of the net lease REIT’s cash rent was derived from this market (the remaining 51% was mostly regional and slightly international in Alberta, Canada).

To be clear, I’m not all too worried about the experiential draw of Las Vegas itself (the city has arguably become the entertainment capital of the world). I believe the transformation of Las Vegas into a higher-end market continues to play out.

A risk worth watching tied to this geographic concentration is that the snow water equivalent for the western U.S. was the lowest on record for April 1st. The amount of water stored in western U.S. snowpack is nearly two-thirds (65%) below the 1991-2020 normal. How does this matter for VICI?

It doesn’t matter so much from an indoor operations perspective at its casinos. That’s because almost all water used indoors is treated and returned to Lake Mead, which means net consumption is almost zero.

The water used for outdoor features (fountains, lush landscaping, and pools) is emblematic of Las Vegas itself, and it’s almost completely consumptive. If Lake Mead falls below Tier 2 or Tier 3 levels, the Southern Nevada Water Authority could mandate stricter cuts in water consumption. That could force tenants to spend significant amounts of money to retrofit cooling systems or replace grass with turf, which could put further pressure on their rent coverage ratio.

Summary: A SWAN Holding for Me

VICI’s embedded organic growth through contractual escalations provides a growth tailwind for the net lease REIT. As do its acquisitions, like the Golden Entertainment sale-leaseback, expected to close in mid-2026, and its pending acquisition of a Canadian portfolio from Pure Casino Entertainment. The strategy to act as a capital provider for developments not only generates interest income for VICI but also opens the door to eventually acquiring the underlying real estate. The net lease REIT also boasts an investment-grade balance sheet.

Clinching my Buy case, VICI’s shares are as undervalued now as they were in December. This paves the way for 11% annual total returns for the foreseeable future, which is why I’m comfortable holding VICI as my portfolio’s 10th largest position, with a 2.2% weighting.

data.title: Jackson Financial: A Safe Name Among Private Credit Fears
data.description:
Shares of Jackson Financial (JXN) have been an excellent performer over the past year, gaining over 40%, as strong results have enabled material capital returns. That said, like much of the insurance sector, the stock has struggled more recently, down 20% from its high, as investors contemplate potential private credit losses in portfolios. While this is an important concern, Jackson is actually quite well positioned for potential challenges in this asset class, in my view. I have been a long-term bull on JXN, reiterating shares as a “strong buy” in January following its TPG (TPG) partnership; however, the stock is down 13% since then. With updated financials and recent developments, now is a good time to revisit JXN to see if this pullback is an opportunity.

In recent months, we have seen cracks in private credit build. This has led to $20 billion of redemption requests over the past quarter, and many firms have gated redemptions, given the fact most funds have a cap of 5% per quarter. Private credit, especially direct lending, has taken off over the past 15 years as banks rebuilt capital following the financial crisis and faced new regulations. This created the space for asset managers to lend directly to businesses that still sought loans.

Private credit is not innately more dangerous than public credit, so long as asset managers underwrite loans as rigorously as banks did. However, whenever an asset class grows dramatically, one should be wary that this growth is facilitated by easing lending standards, and direct lending has grown massively. There has been particular concern about software lending, especially with potential business disruption risk from AI. We have now seen several notable defaults where recovery was $0, which has added to alarm.

This is particularly relevant for the insurance industry because insurers are significant owners of private credit. Because their liabilities are long-dated, they can have less liquid portfolios, which increases the attractiveness of private vs. public markets. Plus, particularly in less risky policies (like fixed annuities), insurers make money not on the underwriting, but by having a higher investment yield. With private equity firms like KKR (KKR) and Apollo (APO) owning their own insurance companies, this has increased competition to enhance portfolio yields to remain competitive, requiring more private credit usage.

While a meaningful wave of defaults in private credit (which may or may not ultimately happen) would impact the insurance sector as a whole, not every insurer has a similar amount of exposure. In fact, Jackson had no material exposure to middle market or direct lending before a recently announced TPG partnership. Instead, to the extent it has private credit exposure, it is primarily investment-grade tranches of structured products, with Jackson historically relying on other asset classes, like emerging markets, to enhance its portfolio yield. As of the fourth quarter, it maintained a very conservative portfolio.

Within its corporate portfolio, less than 2% are rated below investment grade. Of its $4.4 billion ABS portfolio, 85% are rated A or higher, and just 1% are below investment grade. Its biggest allocation within this slice of its portfolio is in collateralized loan obligations (“CLOs”), which are structured securities supported by a portfolio of leveraged loans. Here, Jackson is defensively positioned with 93% of the portfolio rated-A or higher and 22% AAA. No AAA CLO has ever defaulted, given its structural seniority. I expect that to remain the case this cycle, and I see very little risk of JXN facing impairment on its CLO portfolio.

As a reminder, in January, Jackson signed an agreement with TPG whereby TPG invested $500 million in Jackson stock, and this deal was completed in February. Under the terms of the deal, JXN is receiving $150 million of TPG stock, so essentially it sold $500 million of stock for $650 million, or a 30% premium. With these proceeds, Jackson is able to seed a new captive reinsurance entity, Hickory Re, with $650 million to reinsure fixed and fixed index annuities. This enhances the capital efficiency of new sales inside its Jackson National Life subsidiary, making it easier to grow the business.

Now, TPG is paying a premium because it will begin investing a sleeve of Jackson’s portfolio. JXN will give TPG $4 billion within 2 years, ramping to $12 billion within 5 years. The contract lasts for five years. This will generate at least $60 million of annual management fees for TPG, which is how it aims to earn back the premium it is functionally paying for JXN’s shares. For Jackson, I view the initial financial terms as attractive. Absent this deal, it would have $500 million less to spend on buybacks (given the capital need for its new reinsurer). This year, when it buys back $500 million of stock, it will have the same share count as before TPG, a new reinsurance entity, and $150 million of TPG stock that it previously did not.

In essence, Jackson’s direct lending and private credit capabilities lagged peers, which could limit the ability to attractively price new policies. As a result, it is partnering with TPG to catch up. Given the fact that outstanding private credit is a source of concern, its lack of exposure is actually a positive, as JXN does not have this risk on its balance sheet.

Now, over time, as the TPG partnership ramps up, it will have more private credit exposure. However, if there is a time to invest in private credit, I would actually argue we are approaching that time. In periods of stress and outflows, there is a chance to buy assets at discounted prices, and if anything, underwriting rigor is likely to be stronger going forward than in the past. Plus, JXN is moving methodically, with only about 5% of its portfolio going to TPG this year.

Ultimately, being behind peers worked out in JXN’s favor, and it should face much lower credit losses than peers should a downturn occur. As such, any sell-off related to private credit fears is an opportunity in my view. This is especially the case because the company is performing at a high level. Looking back to recent results, Jackson delivered a blow-out fourth quarter, earning $6.61, which was $0.75 ahead of consensus.

Retail annuity sales hit a quarterly record in Q4 of $5.9 billion, and 2025 was also the strongest reported since JXN became a public company. In total, sales approached $20 billion. RILA sales were $2.3 billion in Q4 (up 53%) as the company launched an updated offering. Its RILA account values have reached $20 billion, up 14% from last year.

In total, nonvariable annuity flows were a positive $2.8 billion in the quarter, consistent with the company’s long-term strategy of derisking its liabilities. These policies are much less risky (especially relative to historic variable annuity offerings), and the steady growth in these products is reducing the overall riskiness of Jackson’s liabilities, which should pave the way for steadier capital returns. In contrast, variable annuity sales were up a modest 1% to $2.8 billion, and these sales continue to mix to less-risky offerings that do not provide lifetime guarantees. Over time, this should unlock potential multiple expansion, though I do not expect that to occur in a meaningful way until private credit overhangs from the sector are worked through.

Jackson Life maintains a stellar capital position with a risk-based capital ratio of 567%, well in excess of the 400-450% I like to see. With this capital posture, JXN can fund ongoing growth in annuity sales while continuing to dividend capital up to the parent holding company. These intercompany dividends are essential because it is the holding company that does share buybacks and pays the dividend. In 2025, Jackson’s holding company had $838 million of free cash flow, thanks to $1.1 billion of dividends from the operating company, less holding company expenses. The holding company also ended the year with $654 million of cash, in excess of the $500 million I like to see it hold.

During 2025, it repurchased $634 million of stock, reducing its share count by more than 8%. Plus, in February, Jackson also increased its dividend by 13% to $0.90, and shares now offer a 3.5% dividend yield. I expect JXN to continue growing capital returns thanks to durable cash flow and its strong financial positioning. Indeed, alongside Q4 results, JXN provided full-year guidance, expecting at least $1.2 billion of free capital generation alongside $900 million to $1.1 billion of capital returns.

Back in January, I was targeting $700-$750 million of buybacks and about $13.50/share of total capital returns, which is broadly consistent with guidance. That drove my fair value estimate of $135, using a 10x multiple. I still expect buybacks to end up in this area, and capital returns should be at least at that $13.50 level. With a safe investment portfolio and improving liability mix, I view this pace of capital return as sustainable. As such, I view the recent sell-off as an opportunity, and I continue to view JXN as a “strong buy.” I expect results this year to show its resilience to private credit challenges, and that should enable shares to rally, aided by the buyback. With over 30% upside, JXN is very attractive.

data.title: GE Vernova Isn't Priced For What It Has Already Become
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The absence of value has been experienced by the big-cap industrial groups all this time, with the most evident structural mispricing being GE Vernova (NYSE: GEV). The three business ventures that the company is currently undertaking are power, wind, and electrification; all three will undoubtedly benefit from two significant infrastructure build-outs of the present decade: the rise in the use of electricity through AI and the global energy transition. GEV has performed on all of its major Financial metrics—orders, backlog growth, revenue acceleration, margin expansion, and free cash flow generation—since its spin-off from General Electric in April 2024, and the market has continued to value the company using a traditional industrial OEM multiple to a business that is becoming more of a long-cycle infrastructure franchise with multi-year locked-in pricing and recurring services revenue.

The investment thesis is based on three pillars. First, the AI electricity supercycle is structural, not cyclical: U.S. data center power demand will increase 130% between 2024 and 2030, according to the IEA, and the five largest hyperscalers pledged to spend $660–690 billion in combined capital expenditure in 2026 alone—an infrastructure commitment that requires the specific equipment GEV manufactures. Second, GEV's $150 billion backlog entering 2026, combined with Slot Reservation Agreements (SRAs) priced 10-20 points above current backlog pricing, creates a compounding margin improvement that is built into future income statements but not yet visible in the current P&L. Third, the Prolec GE acquisition, completed on February 2, 2026, has made GEV the only company in the world capable of supplying an integrated 'power-to-rack' solution—from gas turbine to distribution transformer to switchgear—from a single supplier to hyperscale customers that are actively requesting exactly this kind of vertical integration.

Analysts covering GEV have a consensus 'Buy' rating based on 34 Wall Street analysts, with a median 12-month price target of $842.50 (ranging from $560 to $1,019), per Ticker Nerd analyst consensus data (April 2026). My independent price target of $1,150 per share reflects a 22x EV/EBITDA multiple on 2027 consensus EBITDA estimates of approximately $7.5–8 billion, a multiple appropriate for an infrastructure franchise with GEV's revenue visibility, free cash flow trajectory, and secular demand tailwinds. This represents approximately 28% upside from the current trading price.

The AI Electricity Supercycle: Why This Demand Is Structural

Although GEV may have an advantage in the data center, it is not the most appropriate way to render the magnitude of the structural change that is taking place in the U.S. electricity system. This concurrent growth of AI infrastructure, industrial electrification, and automation is a more sophisticated manner of expressing the matter. This was caused by the insufficiency in investment in transmission capacity, resulting in the greatest power generation and grid equipment demands since postwar electrification.

The scope of the problem is also established with the help of third-party data, which are credible. The International Energy Agency's Energy and AI report predicts that U.S. data center electricity consumption will grow by approximately 240 TWh between 2024 and 2030. As estimated by the Pew Research Center, the electricity consumption of U.S. data centers was more than 4% of total national electricity consumption, with 183 TWh used in 2024 and 426 TWh estimated by 2030. S&P Global Market Intelligence has estimated that in 2025, U.S. data center grid power demand will be 61.8 GW, and by 2030 it will be 134.4 GW—a figure never before realized on a large-scale basis.

Another sign of the imminence of the need to invest is the capital distribution of the hyperscalers. Futurum Group's AI Capex 2026 analysis estimates that the top five U.S. hyperscalers have already committed $660–690 billion in CapEx for 2026. In 2024, the U.S. electric utility sector alone had an expenditure of about $160 billion on the improvement of its infrastructure in terms of generation, transmission, and distribution. The technology companies are now outspending the utility industry on energy infrastructure by a factor of more than two to one. This has seen the lead time of power transformers increase to 128 weeks. According to Goldman Sachs, data center power demand across the world will increase by 165% by 2030. To fulfill this demand, GEV manufactures equipment that encompasses heavy-duty gas turbines to provide dispatchable baseload power, large transformers to interconnect the grid, and switchgear and substations to provide distribution, as well as HVDC systems for long-distance bulk transfer of power.

This demand cycle renders supply-side substitution virtually impossible, in contrast to most infrastructure boom and bust cycles. The outcome is an oligopoly of firms—GEV, Siemens Energy, and Mitsubishi Power—that manufacture heavy-duty gas turbines with combined-cycle efficiency exceeding 60%, encompassing virtually all the meaningful supply. Specialized metallurgy, precision casting, and prolonged qualification programs with utility operators are required in the manufacturing process. It is a process that new entrants are not able to accelerate. The establishment of additional capacity cannot be achieved in a few months or even a year. Owing to this, the backlog has grown from approximately $100 billion to $150 billion, and management estimates this will amount to ~$200 billion by 2028. The constraint is not demand; the constraint is supply.

The $150 Billion Backlog: Revenue Visibility That Has No Industrial Precedent

During the Q4 2025 earnings call, GE Vernova CEO Scott Strazik demonstrated that the company entered 2026 with a $150 billion backlog—50% larger than the backlog that existed when the GEV spin-off from General Electric was announced in November 2021. The increase in backlog, which stands at approximately 11% per year, has been accompanied by quality improvement, not just volume growth. Between 2025 and 2026, the equipment margin in backlog has gone up by six points, and management has guided a cumulative total of at least $22 billion in equipment margin additions to backlog between 2023 and 2026. GEV is not only accumulating orders at better prices than the current P&L reflects but is also reserving a margin profile materially higher than what is currently visible in the income statement.

The majority of the market commentary does not understand the significance of differentiating between firm backlog and SRAs, and it is the key to the power of forward earnings inherent in the order book. Firm orders are purchase commitments that are binding with fixed delivery dates and fixed prices. SRAs are lower-level commitments where customers place a deposit and commit to a pricing model on an earlier basis and then convert the commitment into firm orders, usually 6–18 months later. As of Q4 2025, GEV held approximately 40 GW of gas turbine firm orders and 43 GW of SRAs—totaling 83 GW of committed or reserved capacity against a current annualized production rate of roughly 16 GW and a mid-2026 target of 20 GW. The pricing on new SRAs is running 10–20 percentage points above the pricing embedded in existing firm backlog. As SRAs convert to firm orders and new SRAs are signed at current market rates, the margin profile of the backlog improves automatically—a self-reinforcing mechanism that management has already confirmed will add 'at least as many equipment margin dollars in backlog in 2026 as in 2025.

For investors who focus on revenue recognition mechanics, GEV does not book the full value of a gas turbine contract as revenue when the order is signed. Revenue is recognized as equipment is manufactured and delivered, typically 18–36 months after contract signature for large, heavy-duty turbines. The $150 billion backlog at year-end 2025 therefore represents contractually committed future revenue—revenue the income statement has not yet seen. With 2025 revenue of $38.1 billion, the backlog-to-revenue coverage ratio stands at 3.9x, implying that GEV has more than three and a half years of revenue fully covered even in a zero-new-orders scenario. That scenario is not happening: Q4 2025 orders totaled $22.2 billion—a book-to-bill ratio of approximately 2x—and management expects to reach approximately 100 GW under total contract by the end of 2026.

Power, Electrification, and the Prolec Catalyst

The three segments of GEV—power, wind, and electrification—each need to be analyzed separately because the risk/reward profile is different across the same corporate structure. A well-known headwind to the industry is due to the combination of U.S. offshore wind stop-work orders and onshore permitting difficulties, which would decrease wind revenue by the low double digits in 2026, with approximately $400 million in segment EBITDA losses. The most effective method to consider wind is that it is a contained and, to a large extent, a shielded drag—and therefore, any possible stabilization or recovery is a good upside optionality that fits into the zero current consensus model.

All this is fueled by the profitability of the power segment. Full-year 2025 Power orders increased by 52% on an organic basis, with 41 heavy-duty gas turbines and 18 aeroderivative units being delivered in Q4 alone. Management anticipates achieving organic growth of 16–18% in Power by 2026 with EBITDA margins also in the 16–18% range—a combination of volume leverage due to the production ramp, improved pricing on new contracts, and productivity improvements already in place in the current manufacturing base. GEV believes it can achieve an annualized gas turbine production of 20 GW by mid-2026 from its current run rate of approximately 16 GW, rising further to 24 GW by 2028. This rise in production that ships against backlog contracts at prevailing prices will result in more revenue and higher segment margin.

The electrification segment is probably the longest and most protracted pillar of the thesis. Revenue grew 32% year-over-year in Q4 2025, with EBITDA margin widening from 13.0% to 17.1%—rapidly approaching the profitability of the Power segment. Q4 2025 orders ran at approximately 2.5x revenue, building its own multi-year backlog base at an accelerating pace. Critically, data centers are now coming directly to GEV as co-creators of the power-to-rack solution, as CEO Strazik noted at the October 2025 Q3 earnings call.

The Prolec GE acquisition, completed February 2, 2026, for $5.275 billion, is the most strategic action that the company has made as a standalone public company. Prolec GE is a large transformer manufacturer with approximately 10,000 employees, seven manufacturing plants in the Americas, and five in the United States. Power transformers are currently the bottleneck in the AI infrastructure supply chain, with a lead time of 128 weeks. The initial acquisition of Prolec by GEV resulted in a conservative estimate of $3 billion in electrification segment revenue added to 2026 guidance, immediately accretive before any operational synergies, and positioned it as the only supplier capable of meeting hyperscaler demands with integrated grid solutions across the full voltage spectrum.

Valuation Analysis: The OEM Framework Versus the Infrastructure Franchise

Although the corporate structure of the organization is similar, the risk/reward profiles within the power, wind, and electrification sectors of GEV will vary, and different analytical methods will be required. Wind is probably a general headwind, and it is a familiar deficiency. The offshore wind stop-work orders issued by the U.S. will become permanent, and the onshore permitting also has concerns. This will cause Wind revenue to drop to the low double digits by 2026, with approximately $400 million in segment EBITDA losses. Think of Wind as a closed and shielded drag—any stabilization or recovery will be an upside on the zero current consensus model.

GEV's forward valuation cannot be assessed correctly using traditional industrial OEM multiples. It is better characterized by multi-year backlogs, SRA pricing, a global installed base, and co-development deals with hyperscalers. A long-cycle franchise that generates cash predictably is more comparable to an infrastructure investor's framework than to a cyclical equipment manufacturer that is subject to late-cycle order decay. The market is not asking the right question—whether 39x 2026 EV/EBITDA is high for an industrial is not the issue. What should be asked is, what should a $150 billion locked-demand infrastructure franchise with a 20% EBITDA margin target by 2028 and $24 billion of committed free cash flow be worth against the 2027–2028 earnings base?

GEV currently trades at approximately 33x 2026 EV/EBITDA at the midpoint of guidance. This appears elevated against a traditional industrial peer set—but the comparison is structurally misleading. When the same enterprise value is applied to the 2028 EBITDA target of 20% on $56 billion revenue—implying approximately $11.2 billion of EBITDA—the implied 2028 EV/EBITDA falls to approximately 20x on a business growing EBITDA by more than 100% from 2026 levels under management-confirmed guidance. A 20x multiple is hardly aggressive for a company with earnings growth anchored to contractually committed backlog margins of 16–18% in power and 17–19% in electrification.

Risk Factors

Offshore Wind Policy and Execution Drag

The most prominent danger is the wind sector. The impact of the work order suspension issued by the current administration on offshore wind in the U.S. is, however, significant. The offshore wind development plans of the company's business in the U.S. are anticipated to incur EBITDA losses of up to $400 million. The management appears to be control-oriented, but the policy front is not clear, and in case the administration were to extend the offshore wind restrictions to cover international projects or enable onshore permitting, the wind would still drag. The risk is partly offset by the wind losses that have already been factored into guidance conservatism and the development of indications of the revival in U.S. onshore wind repowering: in 2025, GEV's Onshore Wind division was assigned the responsibility of repowering 1.1 GW of onshore turbines.

Gas Turbine Production Ramp Timing

The most important operational milestone in a shorter time period and applicable to any investment thesis is the goal of generating 20 GW per year. The risk of execution in capital-intensive industries is caused by the availability of skilled labor, constraints of the supply chain of specialized alloys, and equipment qualification timelines, among others. The potential revenue recorded on Power backlogs may fall substantially between mid-2026 and Q4 2026 or early 2027, causing missed quarterly earnings. The risk of late deliveries is mostly self-remedying—provided the demand does not decrease faster than the supply, revenue is deferred but not lost.

Tariff and Trade Policy Exposure

The manufacturing operations of the company are evenly distributed across North America, Europe, and Asia, and in case of an increase in industrial tariffs, it would incur higher costs of acquiring specialized materials such as steel and copper. Crucial mitigants include improved domestic content from its five U.S. manufacturing sites and price escalation provisions in long-term contracts of Prolec GE. As indicated in the Deloitte analysis of U.S. AI infrastructure, developed economies are more concerned with grid infrastructure than with national security, which has resulted in the establishment of tariff systems that penalize manufacturers of energy equipment who profit from home-based income.

Capital Allocation: A Company That Has Changed Its DNA

Nonetheless, the constantly reiterated capital allocation framework that GEV has articulated and executed is a definite sign that their leadership seized the chance of value creation. The company repurchased over 8 million shares and returned $3.6 billion in 2025 while simultaneously funding the $5.3 billion Prolec GE acquisition and investing in gas turbine production capacity expansion across its French and South Carolina facilities. A $200 million investment in a new electrification manufacturing facility has been initiated in Hai Phong, Vietnam. The board raised the share repurchase authorization to $10 billion from the previously approved $6 billion program and doubled the quarterly dividend to $0.50 per share, with the company ending the year with a cash balance of $8.8 billion. The cumulative free cash flow commitment of at least $24 billion from 2025 to 2028—against a current market capitalization of approximately $242 billion—implies GEV will generate roughly 10% of its current market cap per year in free cash flow over the next three years, a metric that is simply not reflected in the current multiple.

The fact that gross leverage has been kept below 1x adjusted EBITDA even after the financing of the $5.3 billion Prolec acquisition—funded equally with cash and new debt—is the opposite of an industrial company trying to adopt similar growth opportunities. The positive balance sheet of the spin will help in accelerating strategic flexibility in case of additional M&A opportunities in grid infrastructure, which will consolidate with time as transformer and switchgear manufacturers acquire strategic benefits in the field of AI power build-out. When the dividend and buyback plan is continued, the strength of GEV can grow. Management has explicitly committed to returning at least one-third of cash generation to shareholders through higher dividends and the increased buyback program—which will produce a floor under the stock price as the FCF trajectory builds.

Bull and Bear Cases

Bull Case: $1,400+ Price Target

The electrification margins will start moving towards the upper end of the range of 17–19% as soon as GEV achieves its production target of mid-2026. The synergies brought about by the acquisition of Prolec GE will be brought to the fore in the Q2 2026 earnings call. When 2027 EBITDA revisions become more than $8.5 billion, the SRA pricing step-up will be reflected in literal sell-side estimates—forcing a rerating into 25× 2027 EBITDA after 2027 EBITDA revisions. And any partial fix to U.S. offshore wind policy headwinds, such as the suspension of the expansion of stop-work orders, would open the door to the eradication of the estimated $400 million a year wind drag by 2027. The value of the enterprise will be about $212 billion, assuming a 25× multiple on $8.5 billion of EBITDA in 2027. GEV has a total debt of about $1.2 billion and cash of about $8.8 billion, with a net cash position of about $7.6 billion. With a diluted stock outstanding of 270 million shares, adding the net cash position to the enterprise value would translate to an equity value of about $219.6 billion, or $1,400 per share (≈ $219.6 billion ÷ 270 million shares).

Bear Case: $650 Price Target

Two interdependent headwinds exist: the first is that the production ramp of gas turbines can be prolonged to late 2026 or early 2027, pushing Power segment revenue below the 16–18% organic growth guidance, and the second is that the U.S. tariff policy will raise the cost of inputs beyond the reimbursable range of the contract escalation clauses, narrowing the EBITDA margins toward the lower end of the 11–13% guidance range. In this case, the 2026 EBITDA would be around $4.5 billion, at the low end of the guidance-implied range, and the market would be beginning to attach a multiple compression of between 25× and 27× as it becomes increasingly clear that execution issues are arising. At 26× EBITDA of $4.5 billion, equity value is approximated at about $124.6 billion, and adding the net cash position of about $7.6 billion to the $117 billion implied enterprise value (26× × $4.5 billion EBITDA) results in a value of about $650 per share (≈ $124.6 billion ÷ 270 million shares). The bear case is unrealistic without both execution and policy headwinds failing at the same time—none of these risks by themselves is sufficient to achieve such a result.

Conclusion: The Power Grid Is The Underpriced AI Infrastructure Layer

The IEA estimates U.S. data center electricity consumption to grow by 130% by 2030. In 2026, the capital expenditure on hyperscalers was between $660 billion and $690 billion, and this corresponds to over four (4) years of the annual spending on infrastructure of the U.S. utility industry. The Microsoft CEO has made it public that they are unable to connect the GPUs with the grid, and this has led to them being held up in stocks. It is not a bottleneck of computation. The answer to the bottleneck is GE Vernova, with a backlog of $150 billion, 83 GW of contracted gas turbine projects, and the only integrated power-to-rack electrification platform in the world.

It is not a prediction of the Financial situation—it is a statement. According to management guides for 2026, adjusted EBITDA margins of 11–13% and revenue of between $44 billion and $45 billion are anticipated. The current 3.9x annual revenues backlog, with free cash flow of $5.0–$5.5 billion, reflects prices currently 10–20 pts higher than what is visible in the income statement. The management has committed to providing $56 billion in revenue, 20% EBITDA margins, and $24 billion in cumulative free cash flow by 2028. These are not the projections of hedged forward analysts but targets enhanced with more difficult customer commitments at higher prices. The next verification will occur on April 22 with the Q1 2026 earnings call, and it is clear that each successive quarter the ramp turns into a new quarter, making it even harder to rationalize the multiple currently in place.

My 12-month price forecast of $1,150 per share, implying 22x 2027 EBITDA, is up by a respectable 28% from current prices, yet still by far the lowest among analyst estimates on an equal basis. I regard shares as having limited upside because of what I believe to be impractical operations enhancement. The bear case—involving production delays, tariff exposure, and a long WIND drag—has already been taken care of with deliberately conservative management guidance. Such structural elements as SRA conversions at 10–20 point margin premiums, Prolec synergy recognition, and wind segment recovery optionality are absent, which is a peculiarity of the bull case. The backlog of $150 billion printing at 20% EBITDA margins should give investors a clear picture of what is in store. The large-cap value proposition in the AI infrastructure cycle is the one that is not in line with the current prices of GE Vernova, and this is what makes it a viable option.
